content:
# Matthew Chapter 17 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 17 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 17:2 -- "shone like the sun" vs. "blazed like the sun"; "white as light" vs. "dazzling white"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the face of him shone as the sun, and the garments of him became white as the light" | Literal with possessive structure |
| LOGOS | "his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "His face blazed like the sun, and his clothes became dazzling white" | Dynamic intensification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "shone like the sun... white as light." The Greek lampsen means "shone/shined," not "blazed." PNEUMA intensifies both descriptions -- "blazed" is stronger than "shone," and "dazzling white" adds a descriptor not in the Greek (leuka hos to phos = "white as the light"). While the transfiguration is visually dramatic, the translation should not exceed the Greek's own descriptors.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's intensification, while evocative, goes beyond the source.

---

## 17:4 -- "tents" vs. "shelters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will make here three tents" | Literal skenas |
| LOGOS | "I will make three tents here" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "I'll set up three shelters" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "tents." The Greek skene (tent/tabernacle) echoes the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) and the tabernacle of Israel's wilderness wandering. "Shelters" (PNEUMA) loses this rich biblical resonance. The word choice connects Peter's suggestion to Israel's festival calendar and sacred architecture.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. The tabernacle/Sukkot echo is preserved.

---

## 17:5 -- "listen to him" vs. "hear him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "hear him" | Literal akouete autou |
| LOGOS | "listen to him" | Formal equivalent with active connotation |
| PNEUMA | "Listen to him!" | Same as LOGOS with exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "listen to him." The Greek akouete can mean "hear" or "listen to." In this divine command (echoing Deuteronomy 18:15), "listen" conveys the obedience dimension: not merely auditory perception but attentive response. PNEUMA's exclamation mark adds emphasis; SOPHIA retains the semicolon-delimited structure of the voice's declaration.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:15 -- "epileptic" vs. "has seizures"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he is epileptic" | Literal seleniazetai (lit. "moon-struck") rendered as medical term |
| LOGOS | "he is an epileptic" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "He has seizures" | Dynamic -- describes symptoms rather than labeling |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "epileptic." The Greek seleniazomai literally means "to be moon-struck" but was used for epileptic seizures. "Epileptic" is the established English medical/biblical rendering. PNEUMA's "has seizures" describes symptoms but avoids the traditional term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 17:17 -- "twisted" vs. "perverted" vs. "corrupt"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "O faithless and perverted generation" | Literal diestrammene (perverted/twisted) |
| LOGOS | "O faithless and twisted generation" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "What a faithless and corrupt generation!" | Dynamic with "corrupt" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "twisted." The Greek diestrammenon means "distorted, twisted, perverted" (from diastrepho, to twist). "Twisted" captures the distortion without the stronger connotations of "perverted" (EMET), which in modern English often implies sexual deviance. "Corrupt" (PNEUMA) is broader and less precise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:20 -- "little faith" vs. "your little faith" vs. "Because of your little faith"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Because of the little-faith of you" | Literal oligopistian with genitive |
| LOGOS | "Because of your little faith" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Because of your little faith" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators agree on the essential rendering. EMET's structure differs only in word order. No substantive divergence.

**Status:** Agreement across translators.

---

## 17:27 -- "shekel" vs. "stater" vs. "four-drachma coin"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you will find a stater" | Transliterates the Greek coin name |
| LOGOS | "you will find a shekel" | Renders in familiar biblical currency |
| PNEUMA | "you'll find a four-drachma coin" | Explains the coin's value |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "shekel." The stater was a four-drachma coin equivalent to one shekel. "Shekel" is the most recognizable biblical monetary term for English readers. EMET's "stater" requires a footnote for comprehension. PNEUMA's "four-drachma coin" is accurate but clinical.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:24 -- "two-drachma tax" vs. "didrachma" vs. "temple tax"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] receiving the didrachma" | Transliterates Greek coin name |
| LOGOS | "the collectors of the two-drachma tax" | Translates with explanation |
| PNEUMA | "the collectors of the temple tax" | Dynamic -- identifies the tax by function |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "two-drachma tax." This preserves the specific amount while being comprehensible. PNEUMA's "temple tax" is functionally correct but loses the specific monetary reference. EMET's "didrachma" requires glossing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:22 -- "delivered" vs. "betrayed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The Son of the Man is about to be delivered into [the] hands of men" | Literal paradidosthai (to hand over/deliver) |
| LOGOS | "The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "The Son of Man is going to be betrayed into human hands" | Renders paradidomi as "betrayed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "delivered." The Greek paradidomi means "to hand over, deliver, entrust." While Judas' betrayal is in view, the word itself is broader than "betrayed" and is used throughout the passion narrative for the entire chain of hand-overs (Judas to priests, priests to Pilate, Pilate to soldiers). "Delivered" preserves this breadth.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. "Betrayed" is too narrow for paradidomi in this context.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase vs. LOGOS/EMET | 6 | LOGOS preferred |
| EMET transliteration vs. LOGOS standard | 3 | LOGOS preferred (stater, didrachma, Elias) |
| PNEUMA intensifies beyond Greek | 1 | Rejected (17:2 "blazed/dazzling") |
| Biblical/cultural term preserved | 2 | "Tents" (tabernacle echo), "delivered" (broader paradidomi) |
| All three agree | 1 | 17:20 (little faith) |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 17 has fewer disagreements than the preceding chapters, consistent with its narrative-heavy content (transfiguration, healing, temple tax). The most theologically significant divergence is the "tents/shelters" choice in v.4, where preserving the skene/tabernacle connection to Sukkot and Israel's sacred architecture is essential. The "delivered/betrayed" distinction in v.22 has implications for the entire passion narrative -- SOPHIA preserves the broader paradidomi to avoid prematurely narrowing the term. PNEUMA's visual intensification of the transfiguration scene (v.2) is the most notable case of dynamic rendering overstepping the Greek's own descriptive register. The coin terminology (stater/shekel/four-drachma) represents a recurring project challenge: balancing ancient specificity with reader comprehension.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
